_meta.description: REST API vulnerability knowledge base for APISec scanner.

CORS-001.label: Reflected Origin CORS Misconfiguration
CORS-001.impact: Any malicious website can read this API's responses on behalf of an authenticated victim.
CORS-001.description: The server reflects the attacker-controlled Origin header without validation. Any malicious website can send cross-origin requests to this API and read the response on behalf of an authenticated victim.
CORS-001.solution: Maintain an explicit server-side allowlist of trusted origins. Validate the Origin header against this allowlist before reflecting it. Never reflect arbitrary origins.

CORS-002.label: Wildcard CORS with Credentials Enabled
CORS-002.impact: Complete authentication bypass — attacker receives victim's authenticated session cookies and tokens.
CORS-002.description: The API sets both Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. This combination is explicitly forbidden by the CORS specification and allows any origin to make credentialed cross-origin requests.
CORS-002.solution: Never combine wildcard CORS with credentials. Use a strict origin allowlist if credentials are required.

CORS-003.label: Unrestricted Wildcard CORS Origin
CORS-003.impact: Any website can read this API's responses — data exfiltration risk.
CORS-003.description: The API allows cross-origin requests from any domain via a wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. Any website can read this API's responses.
CORS-003.solution: Restrict CORS to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins.

HDR-001.label: Missing Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
HDR-001.impact: Browsers may connect over plain HTTP, exposing credentials and tokens to network interception.
HDR-001.description: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is absent. Without HSTS, browsers may connect to the API over plain HTTP, exposing credentials and session tokens to network-level interception (man-in-the-middle attacks).

HDR-002.label: Missing X-Content-Type-Options
HDR-002.impact: Browsers may MIME-sniff responses, potentially executing malicious scripts.
HDR-002.description: The X-Content-Type-Options header is absent. Without it, browsers may MIME-sniff responses away from the declared content type, potentially executing malicious scripts disguised as benign content.

HDR-003.label: Missing X-Frame-Options
HDR-003.impact: Clickjacking attacks — users tricked into performing unintended actions.
HDR-003.description: The X-Frame-Options header is absent. This allows the API responses to be embedded in iframes on third-party pages, enabling clickjacking attacks where users are tricked into performing unintended actions.
HDR-003.solution: Add: X-Frame-Options: DENY (or SAMEORIGIN if framing is needed internally)

HDR-004.label: Missing Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
HDR-004.impact: No defense against XSS — malicious scripts can be injected and executed.
HDR-004.description: The Content-Security-Policy header is absent. CSP is a critical defense against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by specifying which sources of content are allowed to be loaded and executed by the browser.
HDR-004.solution: Define a strict CSP policy. Minimum: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'

INFO-001.label: Server Version Disclosure
INFO-001.impact: Attacker can identify known CVEs for the specific server version.
INFO-001.description: The Server header exposes the web server software name and version. This information allows attackers to identify known CVEs targeting the specific version and craft targeted exploits.
INFO-001.solution: Configure the server to return a generic value (e.g. Server: webserver) or suppress the header entirely.

INFO-002.label: Technology Stack Disclosure via X-Powered-By
INFO-002.description: The X-Powered-By header reveals the backend technology and version (e.g. PHP/7.2.1, ASP.NET). This fingerprinting information aids attackers in identifying framework-specific vulnerabilities.
INFO-002.solution: Remove the X-Powered-By header. In Express.js: app.disable('x-powered-by'). In PHP: expose_php = Off.

VERB-001.label: HTTP TRACE Method Enabled
VERB-001.impact: XST attacks — HttpOnly cookies and Authorization headers exposed via reflected request.
VERB-001.description: The HTTP TRACE method is enabled. It reflects the full request back, enabling Cross-Site Tracing (XST) attacks that can expose HttpOnly cookies and Authorization headers.
VERB-001.solution: Apache: TraceEnable Off. Nginx: if ($request_method = TRACE) { return 405; }.

ERR-001.label: Verbose Error / Stack Trace Exposure
ERR-001.impact: Internal details (stack traces, file paths, DB queries, framework versions) aid attacker reconnaissance.
ERR-001.description: The API returns verbose error messages containing stack traces, internal file paths, database error strings, or framework version information. This information is valuable for attackers profiling the backend stack.
ERR-001.solution: Return generic error messages in production. Log detailed errors server-side only. Use error masking middleware.

AUTH-001.label: Endpoint Accessible Without Authentication Token
AUTH-001.impact: Protected resources accessible to unauthenticated users.
AUTH-001.description: This endpoint returns data without any authentication token. Protected resources are accessible to unauthenticated users.
AUTH-001.solution: Enforce authentication middleware on every protected route. Validate the token before any business logic executes.

AUTH-002.label: Endpoint Accepts Invalid / Forged Token
AUTH-002.impact: Token signature, structure, and content are not validated — trivial authentication bypass.
AUTH-002.description: The server returns 200 with a completely invalid token. Token signature, structure, and content are not validated.
AUTH-002.solution: Validate token signature, expiry, issuer, and audience on every request. Return 401 for any token that fails validation.

AUTH-003.label: JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted
AUTH-003.impact: Complete authentication bypass — any attacker can forge arbitrary tokens without a secret key.
AUTH-003.description: The server accepted a JWT with alg:none — no cryptographic signature. Any attacker can forge arbitrary tokens without knowing any secret key. This is a complete authentication bypass.
AUTH-003.solution: Explicitly reject alg:none. Hardcode the expected algorithm server-side. Never trust the algorithm from the token header.

AUTH-004.label: JWT Algorithm Confusion (RS256 -> HS256)
AUTH-004.impact: Attacker can forge any token without knowing the private key.
AUTH-004.description: Server accepted a token where the algorithm was changed from RS256 to HS256, signed with the public RSA key as HMAC secret. Attacker can forge any token without knowing the private key.
AUTH-004.solution: Hardcode the expected algorithm server-side. Use: jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=['RS256']). Never read the algorithm from the token header.

AUTH-005.label: No Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint
AUTH-005.impact: Unlimited brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks possible.
AUTH-005.description: The login endpoint does not enforce any rate limiting. An attacker can send unlimited authentication attempts without being blocked or slowed down, enabling brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.
AUTH-005.solution: Return HTTP 429 after N failed attempts. Add Retry-After, X-RateLimit-* headers. Consider account lockout and CAPTCHA for suspicious IPs.

SQLI-001.label: SQL Injection
SQLI-001.owasp: API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization
SQLI-001.impact: Full database compromise — data exfiltration, authentication bypass, RCE.
SQLI-001.description: SQL injection detected. User-supplied input is incorporated into SQL queries without parameterization, allowing an attacker to manipulate the query logic.
SQLI-001.solution: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements exclusively. Never interpolate user input directly into SQL strings.
